text: The surgeon frees the terminal ileum from adhesions in the right pelvic sidewall, allowing for easier reflection of the small bowel during the procedure.
The surgeon performs a lateral to medial dissection during the colectomy, avoiding high ligation of the vasculature.
The surgeon assesses the colon and determines that adequate length cannot be achieved without mobilizing the splenic flexure.
The surgeon progresses towards the splenic flexure, continuing the dissection along the colon.
The surgeon continues to work upwards towards the flexure, manipulating the tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses scissors to perform the lateral dissection, carefully cutting through tissue layers.
The surgeon switches to a vessel sealer as the dissection progresses higher along the lateral aspect of the colon.
The surgeon demonstrates the approach from the most medial aspect during the colectomy procedure, focusing on the dissection technique.
The surgeon lifts the mesenteric fascia, exposing underlying structures during the dissection process.
The surgeon positions the instruments under the pancreas and near the colonic flexure, preparing for further dissection in this area.
The surgeon performs the lateral approach, visible in the video, followed by the super mesocolic approach during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the technique of approaching the splenic flexure by repeatedly moving medially, navigating towards the area beneath the pancreas.
The spleen is visible in the surgical field as the surgeon continues the mobilization of the splenic flexure during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon observes a high colonic flexure during the procedure.
The surgeon encounters the colon adhered to the surrounding tissue near the splenic flexure, requiring careful separation.
The surgeon dissects across the surgical field, taking down attachments to expose the pancreas, revealing its anatomical position relative to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues to work medially, gradually exposing the high flexure of the colon, with the spleen visible nearby.
The surgeon removes the attachments from the pancreas, revealing the organ's surface as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon encounters the omentum over the top of the surgical field, which is impeding further progress in the dissection.
The surgeon demonstrates the complete mobilization of the colon by performing a lateral splenic flexure release, allowing the colon to be freed from surrounding attachments.
The surgeon prepares to enter the lesser sac to perform complete mobilization of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon prepares to take down the fistula, which is the final step before addressing the mesentery.
The surgeon prepares for transrectal extraction, ensuring the patient will have only four visible eight-millimeter port sites post-procedure.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection to separate the remaining saline mesentery from the sidewall, using careful manipulation to detach the tissue.
The surgeon observes the colon adhering to the abdominal wall along the path to the drain, indicating a possible area of inflammation or previous surgical intervention.
The surgeon removes the drain and proceeds with the lateral mobilization of the colon, detaching it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully medializes the mesentery, adjusting its position to ensure unobstructed access for further surgical steps.
The mesentery is observed closely adhering to the bowel wall without affecting its length.
The surgeon prepares to divide the mesentery at the previously chosen proximal margin, indicating the start of mesenteric division.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection into the thickened mesentery, a process described as more tedious compared to high ligation.
The surgeon uses multiple firings of the vessel sealer on the mesentery to ensure hemostasis, addressing potential immediate or delayed bleeding.
The surgeon advances through the newly created window in the mesentery, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers along the bowel wall, maintaining close proximity to avoid damaging surrounding structures during the mesentery division.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers along the bowel wall, avoiding high ligations and preserving nearby nerves to prevent potential impacts on erection and ejaculation. The technique also minimizes the bulk of the mesentery for easier extraction.
The surgeon addresses delayed bleeding from the previously cut mesentery using a bipolar device, which effectively controls the bleeding.
The surgeon continues to dissect down towards the upper rectum, maintaining close proximity to the bowel wall to avoid high ligations and preserve surrounding nerve structures.
The surgeon has cleared the mesentery on the rectal side and now moves back to the top to ensure the mesentery is cleared proximally.
The surgeon checks the ICG fluorescence to assess perfusion and marks the intended resection margin on the colon.
The surgeon checks the distal section of the colon and marks the surgical margin.
The surgeon uses white light to ensure clear visualization of the surgical field and confirm the location of the marked margin after the ICG has washed out.
The surgeon proceeds to divide the bowel using surgical instruments, ensuring the bowel is separated at the marked margin.
The surgeon uses scissors in cut mode to dissect tissue, occasionally employing cautery in areas prone to bleeding.
The surgeon divides the specimen into smaller pieces for extraction, opting not to use the Alexis wound protector, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon removes residual pieces of fat and places them into the rectum, cleaning up the surgical field after specimen extraction.
The surgeon continues to clean the surgical area, removing any remaining tissue fragments.
The surgeon prepares to perform a sewn end-to-end anastomosis, aligning the two ends of the bowel for suturing.
The surgeon places two silk sutures, with one visible in arm number one being held up, and the other positioned directly in the posterior midline.
The surgeon performs an over-and-over suture on the posterior side, ensuring the mucosa is properly positioned. Anteriorly, the canal technique is used to invert the tissue.
The surgeon uses three or six inch V-lock sutures starting in the posterior midline, stitching outward and around the left side of the anastomosis site.
The surgeon demonstrates each suture bite in detail during the process of creating a sewn end-to-end anastomosis.
The surgeon turns the surgical field to begin suturing the anterior canal, following the placement of silk sutures in the posterior midline.
The surgeon begins to canal out of the corner, ensuring not to cover the posterior midline, which remains visible for the start of the second suture.
The surgeon takes alternating bites of tissue with the suture, using either the right or left hand as needed, to ensure precise placement.
The surgeon begins suturing at the posterior midline using a three or six inch V-lock suture, progressing towards the patient's right side.
The surgeon catches a portion of the mesentery in the stitch and carefully maneuvers it around to avoid obstruction.
The surgeon utilizes the same port configuration as at the start of the procedure, focusing on the area where the splenic flexure was previously taken down.
The surgeon maneuvers the bowel canal anteriorly over the right corner, adjusting its position for better access.
The surgeon observes the well-vascularized corners or edges of the bowel, indicating healthy blood supply to the tissue.
The bowel shows good integrity, with sutures holding the tissue securely in place.
The surgeon removes the needles from the abdominal cavity and closes the right lower quadrant port to prevent potential hernia formation.
The surgeon brings two sutures from both corners anteriorly to meet at the posterior midline, avoiding the corners for a more secure closure.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the newly constructed anastomosis, checking for any signs of fluid leakage around the sutured area.
The surgeon inserts a 19mm practice scope through the anastomosis, observing the absence of air bubbles, indicating a successful seal.
The surgeon removes needles and sponges from the surgical field, then uses a suture passer to close the right lower quadrant eight millimeter port to prevent hernia formation.
The surgeon directs the scope towards the splenic flexure to clear effluent that has traveled upwards in that direction.
